raw_payload.author: masonsaul
raw_payload.content:
Hoppers is a funny and heartfelt animated movie that's more willing than most to acknowledge the violent truth of the circle of life in a darkly comedic fashion and people's inability to change before it backpedals to a satisfying ending focused on how everyone needs to work together to help undo the damage done to nature which still works. It establishes a firm emotional core straight away, goes to truly wild places and is definitely kinda like Avatar in the best ways.

Piper Curda gives a fantastic leading performance that has all the necessary energy to match every insane scenario on screen and an earnest emotion ensuring her desire to save the environment everyone else seems to ignore is instantly investing. Bobby Moynihan is the strongest supporting player by having an unwavering kindness which becomes his character's greatest strength and there's also some strong turns from Jon Hamm, Dave Franco and Eduardo Franco.

Daniel Chong's energetic direction moves quickly at the start but knows when to slow down afterwards with impactful moments of serenity and the screenplay by Jesse Andrews handles exposition so efficiently. The animation retains the characteristically high level of detail and vibrancy in places whilst successfully experimenting with some more cartoonish character designs. Mark Mothersbaugh's score is a highlight that works because it's just as lively as everything else.

raw_payload.author: pugpool10
raw_payload.content:
If it wasn't already obvious, we should be giving fresh talent more opportunities to collaborate with creative legends in order to tell meaningful stories for the next generation. And Hoppers does a great job at that.

It starts off a tiny bit slow, but stick with it because some of the stuff they set up over time truly pays off, it's all so funny and terrifying and personal and sweet and sure to be a future classic with how everything blends together. All hail Daniel Chong!!!!

raw_payload.author: TapchantRandom
raw_payload.content:
Hoppers is a triumph of creativity, humor, and heart. From the very beginning, the film grabs your attention with its unique premise - Mabel, a young animal lover, transferring her consciousness into a robotic beaver to communicate with animals and save their habitat. It's such a fresh concept that Pixar makes feel entirely natural and fun. The animation is breathtaking, filled with vibrant colors, incredible detail, and lively sequences that make the world feel alive. Every frame is thoughtfully crafted, from the playful antics of the animals to the imaginative design of the human-robot interactions.

The voice cast is phenomenal. Piper Curda brings charm and emotion to Mabel, while Bobby Moynihan adds perfect comic timing. Jon Hamm and Meryl Streep elevate their roles with a mix of humor, warmth, and depth that makes every scene enjoyable. The supporting characters are equally memorable, creating a rich ensemble that feels authentic and engaging.

What makes Hoppers truly special is its balance of humor and heart. The jokes land for all ages, from clever puns to physical comedy, while the emotional beats hit without ever feeling forced. The film also subtly weaves in themes of empathy, environmental stewardship, and the importance of connection - all without being preachy.

The pacing is excellent, keeping the story exciting while allowing moments to breathe and develop emotionally. By the end, you feel genuinely invested in the characters' journey and care about their world. The soundtrack complements the animation perfectly, enhancing both the fun and tender moments.

Overall, Hoppers is Pixar at its finest: inventive, hilarious, and deeply touching. It's a film that families can enjoy together, but also one that adults will appreciate for its creativity and emotional resonance. It leaves you laughing, thinking, and feeling - an unforgettable adventure that stays with you long after the credits roll.

raw_payload.author: Pjtaylor-96-138044
raw_payload.content: 'Hoppers (2026)' takes a little while to get going, but it's eventually a really fun, cute and even fairly emotional adventure that's actually a tiny bit unconventional in its plotting. Perhaps it's minor Pixar, particularly in terms of its humour-to-heart ratio, but it's potentially more straightforwardly enjoyable than a lot of their more recent efforts (the difference between it and 'Elio (2025)' is night and day). The animation is lively, colourful and peppered with anime-inspired flair (most notably the protagonist's hair and its ability to flex itself when she gets angry), and the voice work is as solid as you'd expect from an ensemble put together by this studio. The world is refreshingly realistic in its approach to the harsh circle of life inherent to nature's ecosystems, and some of its plot beats are surprisingly bold in their bluntness and their almost brutality. It's also rather funny at times, and should keep you in good spirits even during its least laugh-out-loud segments, and its emotional resonance works nicely potentially precisely because it's more subdued and naturalistic than customary for the studio. It's well-paced, well-designed, well-intentioned, and, well, just quite lovely. Like I say, there's a sense that this is minor Pixar, but it's quite major in terms of modern animated family films. There arguably isn't all that much more to say, but that doesn't mean the picture isn't worth your time. It's entertaining from start to finish, and I had a really good time with it.

raw_payload.author: freesancocho
raw_payload.content: One of the most exciting and action packed movies I've seen from Disney! An emotional roller coaster as we see the life of the protagonist develop. As most Disney films, it is very family friendly. Not only family friendly but also very entertaining. With a touch of science fiction and top of the line visuals, as always by Pixar! It is completely worth the watch on the Big Screen!

raw_payload.author: imdbfan-0977556186
raw_payload.content:
Though this movie has an absolutely insane premise, it definitely works with it. It's one of Pixar's funniest movies in a long time with a steady flow of effective jokes and a running joke involving emojis that had me in tears laughing. Of course, because it's Pixar, the animation is outstanding both in terms of the slapstick and the environments with the nature setting giving the animators a lot to work off of. It's well voice acted on top of that, with Piper Curda leading a very funny cast of modern comedians, SNL alumni and a small appearance by the legendary Meryl Streep. I wouldn't say the movie has one fatal flaw, but it does struggle with how to carry the premise sometimes and the second act is incredibly aimless. There's also a few jokes that miss and miss hard, like an egregious minute-long sequence of the animals making siren noises. I also think the environmental message, though vital to the movie, could have been less heavy-handed, a criticism Pixar also faced with WALL-E.

Overall, this movie is what happens when you try to make The Wild Robot and wind up making Over the Hedge. With that said, I like Over the Hedge, so I do recommend this. Kids are going to have a blast - with advisory to a few intense moments in the climax - while adults will go back and forth between groaning and laughing.

raw_payload.author: YUMENGZ

The film has this effortless charm - the kind that makes you laugh out loud one moment and quietly tear up the next. raw_payload.author: RogueClove
raw_payload.content:
I went into the theatres without any expectations but "Hoppers" hopped into my heart nonetheless. The concept is refreshing and the execution is seamless making it one of the finest pixar originals in a while. raw_payload.author: alexandergotzp
raw_payload.content:
Pixar is back.

Perhaps we have lowered our expectations after a string of underwhelming releases, but Hoopers is a movie you enjoy from start to finish. It reaches into Pixar's full toolkit to make you laugh, care, connect, and simply enjoy the ride.

Yes, it draws obvious inspiration from Avatar, Over the Hedge, and even a little Open Season. Connect the dots and it is not the most original concept on paper. But what the film does well is blend those different tones and story threads into something that feels like its own.

The emotional core is Mabel. She is not a naive teenager. She is in that uncomfortable phase right after university, when you were told for years that you are special, capable, destined to change something. Then real life begins. You send applications. You pitch ideas. You speak up in rooms where no one listens. You realize the world is not waiting for you. It is already moving without you.

It is the moment when idealism meets indifference. When you want to make an impact but discover that impact requires access, structure, influence, and sometimes luck. You feel small. Replaceable. Like you are shouting into a system that does not need your voice. And no one is coming to guide you through it.

That is why her connection with the animal community works so well. In the wild, survival depends on interdependence. Predators and prey still operate within a shared system. Each role matters. Each action has visible consequence. It contrasts with the human world she comes from, where effort often feels invisible

The film is not without flaws. Maybe I am just getting old at 25, but it does feel rushed in places. It is peppered with quick, throwaway jokes that seem engineered for the TikTok generation rather than earned through the story. The film also incorporates some Asian animation influences in style and pacing. Subtle touches that enthusiasts will appreciate, though others may not even register them.

There are flashbacks that hint at greater emotional depth, but the way they are scattered throughout the film dilutes their impact. A more intentional structure could have made those moments land harder.

Still, what excites me most is what Hoopers represents. A new generation of talent brewing inside Pixar, and a film that feels like a bridge between the studio's golden era and wherever it is heading next. It is not the slow-burning, high-stakes, intellectually layered Pixar of old. But it is warm, funny, heartfelt, and genuinely recommendable.

Go watch it.

raw_payload.author: southdavid
raw_payload.content:
After some underwhelming times for Disney/Pixar 's original films in the past few years, "Hoppers" seems to be the first one in a while that was both properly advertised ahead of its realise and receiving critical acclaim. Whilst I didn't dig it as much as some others, I did think, particularly the second half, was reasonably good.

Desperate to save a glade that enshrines happy memories for her of her late grandmother, Mabel (Piper Curda) learns that her biology professor Dr Fairfax (Kathy Najimy) has developed the technology to "hop" their consciousness into robotic animals and live amongst them. Mabel intends to use the tech to encourage a beaver to return to the glade and rebuild the dam, thwarting to currently planned construction, but instead she inspires a full-scale rebellion against Mayor Jerry Generazzo (Jon Hamm) and humans in general.

The first half of "Hoppers" I'd say I found amiable enough stuff. There's a strong environmental message and I liked Piper Curda's performance as Mabel, a headstrong young woman butting heads with Jon Hamm's Jerry. I will say that I thought the second half of the film, from the council of animals onwards to be much stronger though. I really liked Dave Franco's unhinged performance as Titus, son of the insect queen and I enjoyed where the story went.

I thought the art style was fine, if perhaps not particularly inspirational or new. The fire effects in the second half are good though and I enjoyed the reveal of the apex predator that the animals choose to go after Jerry.

Maybe it suffers a little in comparison to "The Wild Robot" which had some similar elements but ultimately was a fair bit better. Judged on its own though "Hoppers" was reasonably good. Probably the best new animated film I've seen so far this year, but we've an awful long way to go.

raw_payload.author: DavidTheMovieGuy
raw_payload.content:
Quite possibly the funniest Pixar film to date.

Unfortunately, I do think that the movie becomes a little too absurd for it's own good. I mean, the premise is inherently a bit silly, but I definitely think that the story jumps the shark at one point. Literally.

Still, this is a beautifully animated film with a nice message. Sure, that message may be a little heavy-handed, but it's not like the movie is going for subtly here. Hoppers fully embraces it's environmental themes (and it's whacky story), which is something that I do respect.

Plus, as someone who happens to be a huge animal lover, I can't deny that a handful of scenes did make me get a little emotional.

So, while the movie may not be one of Pixar's best films, it still happens to be a delightful and entertaining feature.

raw_payload.author: andrewbrudenell1
raw_payload.content: I've found a lot of Pixar's output in the last 5 years to be uncompelling and/or preachy and very rarely funny. This film is...fine. It has a leaner and more focussed story, with lighter, breezier themes aimed more squarely at younger children. Crucially, it is fairly funny and while it may lack a lot of the adult friendly humour of their top tier output, it has likeable characters, a clear message rather than an obnoxious agenda and it kept me engaged through a sensible runtime. It's no classic and I doubt it's going to prove that memorable but it's well made and animated as well as being a step up from recent efforts. If this feels like Pixar going back to basics then hopefully the studio can build on this to course correct away from their recent output.

raw_payload.author: moviesfilmsreviewsinc
raw_payload.content:
Pixar's recent film, Hoppers, showcases the studio's continued presence in pop culture, although its reputation has waned since its peak in the 1990s and 2000s. The plot follows Mabel, a college student and animal lover determined to save a glade from demolition led by the mayor of Beaverton, Jerry Generazzo. Mabel's adventure, which involves being placed inside a robotic beaver, leads her to communicate with animals and uncover the true reasons behind their absence from the glade. The film, while not on par with Pixar's esteemed classics like Finding Nemo or The Incredibles, maintains a lively pace and explores various themes, particularly Mabel's desire for control in her chaotic life. A standout character is King George, the kind-hearted ruler of the mammal kingdom, portrayed with depth by Bobby Moynihan. His optimistic view and supportive relationship with Mabel add emotional layers to the story, despite predictable plot points. However, some narrative aspects suffer from a lack of logical consistency, such as the concept of "pond rules" governing the animal kingdom, which feel naïve and oversimplified. The film's credibility falters due to its inability to convincingly establish its world, lacking the internal consistency seen in other Pixar hits. The narrative's surprise villain and Mabel's role in raising awareness among animal monarchs are presented in ways that dilute their impact. Nevertheless, Hoppers delivers humor and impressive direction, culminating in a satisfying resolution for both Mabel and King George. While it may not achieve the status of a Pixar masterpiece, Hoppers offers an entertaining experience for families, securing its place in the studio's repertoire of enjoyable films. Pixar's latest animated film, Hoppers, builds on themes introduced in a post-credit scene from their 2025 movie Elio, offering a blend of humor, compassion, and emotional depth. The narrative explores the intersection of technology and nature, reminiscent of Chris Sanders' The Wild Robot. The story follows Mabel (voiced by Piper Curda), a passionate animal protector whose childhood escapades lead her to her grandmother's serene glade, igniting her conservationist spirit. As a college student at Beaverton University, Mabel confronts Mayor Jerry Generazzo (Jon Hamm), who plans urban expansion into areas crucial for local wildlife, including her grandmother's cherished glade. Mabel discovers a technology called Hoppers, developed by Dr. Sam (Kathy Najimy), Nisha (Aparna Nancherla), and Conner (Sam Richardson), that allows humans to transfer their consciousness into realistic creature robots. This premise sends Mabel into the body of a beaver, unleashing a series of adventures centered on environmentalism and the human impact on nature, viewed through the eyes of its animal characters. Throughout her journey, Mabel seeks to establish a new population of beavers, a keystone species vital for the ecosystem of her glade. She meets King George (Bobby Moynihan), a mammal monarch who provides sanctuary for animals displaced by human activity. Mabel is joined by a cast of memorable sidekicks, including the laid-back beaver Loaf (Eduardo Franco), the energetic Tom Lizard (Tom Law), and the gruff grizzly Ellen (Melissa Villaseñor), as they devise a plan to oppose Mayor Jerry.

The film features an animal council resembling an assembly of superheroes, where various species, including the formidable Insect Queen (Meryl Streep) and her unpredictable caterpillar son, Insect Prince Titus (Eman Abdul-Razzak), convene to strategize against the encroaching threat. Hoppers thus weaves a rich tapestry of environmental themes, technology's role in society, and the vital balance between human progress and the preservation of the natural world.

raw_payload.author: DerinC-9
raw_payload.content: The first film I saw in theaters this year, and it is a great one to start with. The plot and story were easy enough to follow and all the characters were really likeable. I also enjoyed how the main human antagonist wasn't made out to be a completely heartless evil CEO type, and was given some genuine moments showing him being a good person. It was a nice change of pace considering how those sorts of characters are normally portrayed. The main villian, the butterfly guy Titus, does kinda come out of nowhere and could probably have used a bit more time to set up I suppose, but that really didn't take away from my enjoyment of the film. I found myself wanting to see more of this world and characters after the credits started, which isn't a feeling I have often about a movie.

raw_payload.author: twistedgiraff3
raw_payload.content:
This movie is a phenomenal mix of funny, heartfelt, and tragic. I'd have to imagine almost everyone in the theater shed a tear or two. On the whole I felt like this was a courageous movie for Pixar and Disney to make, it's very weird, it's very modern, and it has a lot of creativity. Not what I expected, but I really enjoyed it.

The movie speaks to themes of environmental conservatism. I think that in certain minor aspects, it fell short of that, but I wholeheartedly agree with the main message that we can coexist with nature.

raw_payload.author: MovieWoof
raw_payload.content:
Hoppers is a technically impressive film that feels like a bit of a departure for Pixar. The premise is fun-undercover robot beavers-but the execution feels a lot more political than the studio's usual output. While the animation is beautiful and the voice cast (especially Jon Hamm and Bobby Moynihan) is great, the tone is a bit of a mixed bag.

The "human mimicry" in the later stages of the film is genuinely unsettling in a way that the animal stuff isn't, which gives the climax a strange energy. It's a solid enough movie, but it feels like the studio is experimenting with a new direction that might benefit from a bit more focus on the "fun" and a bit less on the messaging. A good watch for fans of the studio, but maybe not the instant classic I was hoping for.

raw_payload.author: Quiettownlulu78

raw_payload.author: XingPingH
raw_payload.content:
This movie was a huge disappointment. The environmental theme feels extremely shallow and underdeveloped. Aside from the main character having a brief moment of realization, the film never really addresses human responsibility or consequences.

The protagonist herself is frustrating to watch. She constantly makes impulsive decisions without thinking, yet the story barely allows her to face the consequences or grow in any meaningful way. Her character arc feels forced rather than earned.

Visually and narratively, the film also feels like a collage of familiar ideas from other animated movies rather than something original. Scenes and tropes are stacked together without forming a compelling story.

Worst of all, the humor simply doesn't land. Instead of being funny, many moments feel awkward and uncomfortable.

Overall, the film feels messy, shallow, and surprisingly uninspired.

raw_payload.author: Jelisei
raw_payload.content:

It plays safe. Upper-mid energy. You watch it. You laugh (a bit). You like it. And then... you forget it. No afterimage. No pull to revisit.

It could've been chaotic and iconic. Instead, it stayed comfortable.

Good. But not special.

raw_payload.author: cricketbat
raw_payload.content: Hoppers is a cute kid's movie. I thought the story was interesting and the animation was well-done. The beavers looked so fluffy! However, I think they leaned a little too much into the aggressive nature of Mabel's personality. It kind of comes across as abrasive at times. raw_payload.author: danklear-621-134046
raw_payload.content:
More than a return to form, Hoppers felt like a statement of purpose from the studio that has been struggling to find it as of late (though I do believe their pandemic-era works, specifically Luca, Soul, and Turning Red were fantastic). The first Pixar films all had a rebelliousness alongside heart, and over time they became centered more on the heart. We're back in the rebel phase.

What I felt, specifically through the first two acts of Hoppers, was that animators who grew up on Ghibli now have the keys to the car. This film is American to it's core, to be sure, in its pacing and its tone, but there are moments of pause, peace, and attention to detail that are unique for American animation. It's also frankly terrifying in the third act, which Pixar has been playing with here and there (Elio tried this out too), but they've finally gotten the family horror elements down (the shadow of the flying shark was a masterpiece).

Go see it.

raw_payload.author: daisukereds
raw_payload.content:
A lonely girl is tired of being the only one that cares, but she found solace and peace in nature through her Grandmother. Now she needs all the help she can get to protect her beloved favorite spot.

Saw the Pixar logo, the cute animals in the poster.. went to watch it. I did NOT expect any of this! And looking at the trailer now, I think they gave too much away.

Anyway! I don't think this is not Disney's needed "next hit franchise".. as it goes a little too far in places (jumpscare included). It gets too scary and silly, which is great! But it doesn't have that "timelessness".. but it is a darn good movie!! With some good characters (+ moments), decent pacing, interesting premise and visual elements to accompany these ideas (the shift between non and talking animals), and some very good emotional scenes! It reminded me of UP in more ways than one (maybe it is even as a prequel to it). The style used isn't anything to write about, as the models are serviceable, but there were some visual treats here and there.

As an adult, I had fun and quite a few laughs, but the random kid sitting next to me didn't even chuckle once and the theater was kind of quiet throughout. It is hard to gauge based on my experience, but I think this will be liked and forgotten as time goes by. I would still recommend it to people near my age group that like this sort of stuff.. Kids? I'm not so sure.

raw_payload.author: CEM2021
raw_payload.content:
Hoppers starts off as a genuinely charming movie about a young girl whose love of nature is inspired by her grandmother. Going in, we all know she eventually turns into a beaver, very Avatar-style, so there is already an expectation that the movie will lean into some major silliness.

The first half really works. It is funny, sweet, and genuinely entertaining. I laughed a lot. The humour lands without trying too hard.

But somewhere around the halfway mark, it completely loses the plot.

Instead of building on the heart of the story, it spirals into chaotic, over-the-top antics. It almost feels like the makers are trying to go as bonkers as possible, as if legit, easy-paced storytelling is not enough anymore and constant stimulation is required to hold kids' attention.

The shift is jarring and undercuts what was shaping up to be something thoughtful and sweet.

That is what makes it frustrating. Disney and Pixar should keep taking risks on original stories rather than revisiting old movies. Lately, it feels like the ambition is there, but the execution is not.

raw_payload.author: FilmAppraiser
raw_payload.content: The animation is fine, and the first half hour is calm and promising. At first, the forest setting and slower pacing suggest something charming or meaningful may be taking shape. After that, the film becomes louder, busier, and sillier, with weak humor and limited emotional or narrative depth. While not terrible, it remains fairly shallow and not especially impressive overall.

raw_payload.author: alistmoviereviews
raw_payload.content: I was able to get into the early screening so hopefully this finds you before opening weekend. This is Pixars latest creation and it did not disappoint. The story of mable and a young girl, with a love of animals, who grew up with her grandma . The story starts off like a Typical Disney type movie. Quick intro to the characters and story, with the usual sadness that leaps are main character, mable, into action. The story was excellent as was the script writing. The voices for the characters fit the character they were portraying. The movie had a wholesome message about over population encroaching into and displacing some of our wild fury friends and showed the impact of doing so. As the usual as well, the movie ends on a positive note. Stick around after the movie for a mid and post credits scene. Nether post credit scene seems to introduce anything new but they are still fun to watch. The movie should appeal to children and their adult counterparts alike. Enough to make the kids and parents laugh at many scenes. Overall this is feel good entertainment that is a fun uplifting crowd pleaser for all ages. This will get a solid 9/10. Grab your kids and go watch.

raw_payload.author: timeblank
raw_payload.content:
Hoppers may appear to have some pacing issues, but it's a heartfelt, comedic, and unsettling Pixar movie.

The heartwarming scenes revolve around the Grandmother and Mabel relationship. They're very touching and remind me of my 90 year old dad, who recently passed away. I have a soft spot for the elderly, and when Mabel's grandmother passed, I felt that connection.

The comedic value was so great that there were even some dark moments. I thoroughly enjoyed the movie.

Lastly, the unsettling scene, especially the villain, was so unhinged that I was surprised Pixar actually made it possible. In the end, it was a memorable and most entertaining film that truly impressed me. I give it a 10/10.

raw_payload.author: keikoyoshikawa

And there are plenty of hilarious moments for parents to enjoy, even if the kids might not (fully) understand. The dialogues are pretty good, too.

The past few movies from Pixas had been mediocre. I hope "Hoppers" portents a better crop of films to come.

raw_payload.author: Jope1

raw_payload.author: rannynm
raw_payload.content:
From the very first scene, Hoppers jumps straight into the kind of fun, heart, and adventure that Pixar does best. I was instantly pulled in by the humor, the colorful world, and the lovable characters. Before I knew it, I was fully invested in their journey. What I love most is how the movie balances laugh-out-loud moments with genuine emotion, making it entertaining for kids while still meaningful for adults.

In Disney/Pixar's all-new animated comedy adventure, Hoppers, animal lover Mabel gets the chance to use groundbreaking technology to "hop" her consciousness into a lifelike robotic beaver, allowing her to communicate directly with animals. As she uncovers incredible mysteries of the animal world, she befriends the charismatic beaver King George and must rally the entire animal kingdom to face an imminent human threat: smooth-talking local mayor Jerry (Jon Hamm).

Visually, Hoppers is stunning. The animation is vibrant, detailed, and bursting with life - every frame feels thoughtfully crafted. The character designs are expressive and full of personality, making their emotions feel real and relatable. The voice cast brings incredible energy and heart to the film, perfectly matching both the humor and emotional beats. Bobby Moynihan voices King George, the leader of the pond, and his voice is perfect for the role. Piper Curda shines as Mabel, a passionate animal lover with so much heart, while Dave Franco is hilarious as Titus, a demanding and spoiled character who transforms into the Insect King. The music and sound design elevate key moments throughout the film, making the action exciting and the quieter scenes especially touching.

Hoppers delivers a powerful message about empathy, teamwork, and embracing differences. It reminds us that everyone has a voice and a purpose, even if they do not fit the mold or feel confident at first. It encourages viewers to be brave, to listen to others, and to believe that working together can lead to positive change, making Hoppers not just a fun movie, but a meaningful one that stays with you long after the credits roll.

I give Hoppers 5 out of 5 stars and recommend it for ages 5 to 18, plus adults. You can watch Hoppers in theaters March 6, 2026. By Kennedy P., KIDS FIRST!

raw_payload.author: rgkarim
raw_payload.content:
LIKES: The Animation The Music The Voice Acting The Story The Heart The Comedy The Easter Eggs The Character Development It's Fun

Summary Hoppers is another fine work of animation by the masters of this craft. A blend of realism and anthropomorphizing styles comes together to create a set of sequences that are some of the most impressive balances I've seen in a while. Sure, Zootopia has accomplished much with its animation, but there is often a lean toward the human side rather than the animal. Hoppers chooses to keep the balance much better, finding this odd presentation that is impressive in how much they pack into such simple movements. Seeing the animal characters switch between natural and "natural" form had impressed me with the creativity, and somehow manages to sell both the humor and story elements a little more with the utilization that has a clear-cut purpose and not just haphazard slapstick. Accompanying the animation is a great voice acting group, artists who accomplished amazing things, matching not only the lip-reading, but also the emotional blow that this movie keeps delivering. Piper Curda is a delight, a beacon of a great character that I think will be appreciated by a lot of audiences as she takes such a rebellious character and manages to integrate elements that didn't annoy me, as some of Disney's characters do. Moynihan is funny as ever, but as George, he manages to use his comedic elements and sprinkle in the heartfelt elements that remind me of the newer DuckTales series. It is a powerful combination, especially when you mix it with some of the animation/design elements, and I found him a great co-lead with Curda. As for Hamm, he's as charismatic as ever, bringing his usual businessman style to the film, but toning it down to not be too intense for little ears. The rest do some great things, but I only have so much I can write.

Let's focus on the rest of the movie. Hoppers is a film that dives back into Pixar's elements for storytelling. It's an adventure, loaded with elements that are not the most realistic, but yet have enough anchorage to reality to be completely ridiculous as this adventure plays out. Embedded with some great moral lessons on a lot of hot topic items, Chong and Andrews have brought a multilevel story that has that Disney brand of adventure, while also bringing relevance to the younger generation's needs. The character development actually has some bite to it, a bit more heart to many of the characters that doesn't feel shallow, superficial, or niche like many of their previous films have been stuck with. Mabel, George, Jerry, and so many others hold a lot of quality components that had me laughing, and I quite enjoyed these deeper elements traversing with the rest of the story's heart, especially during those emotionally powerful moves they crafted that might get you nodding. The comedy is great, not too much, nor too kiddy, as Pixar seems to take some risks with making jokes that will appeal to the masses. It certainly is not overstepping the lines or going too adult, but the variety of things they bring up in this film has that well-seasoned cook that satiated my hopes for '90s and 2000s humor to return. Especially the hidden gems they've set up for you to find in this cultural wasteland of background items, cameos, catchphrases, and other references made famous by our Internet/Social Media age (check the backstory on the lizard). All-in-all, the movie is just fun, and one of the most engaging and entertaining times I had in the theater for a Disney movie in quite some time (yes, I'm including Marvel and Star Wars). Thus, this movie turns out to get a lot of points for the enjoyment it brought me.

DISLIKES: The Story is a bit predictable Too Many Characters At Times Too Short of Runtime A Bit Sappy/Preachy

Summary: The movie is not going to be for everyone, and perhaps will still be a bit too safe or unoriginal, given that we aren't creating entirely new worlds or diving into anything as stylish as anime is known to create. However, when you factor that out, the movie's dislikes for me are narrower than I had expected. For one thing, the story is still super predictable; the Disney formula is the primary means to guide us with the usual tropes, trends, and layout that the film does best. There are a few curveballs thrown in, which do help change the dynamic and feel of the movie, but for the most part, you understand what is coming very far down the line with the amount of foreshadowing. However, the bigger element for me is again trying to put too much into a movie and not having the time to do it all in that balanced style. Hoppers has a lot of characters to sort through, and many of them have their moments to shine in the sun, but often are turned into a meme and running joke motif rather than actual inclusion into the story. I still enjoyed the characters, and some of their jokes had me in stitches because of clever writing, but I can't help but think there were too many stories in this film to adequately use them in the ways movies like Toy Story, The Incredibles, and others did so much better. And though someone like the council needed more time, these aren't the characters I'm talking about, but rather ones that have been in the commercials for some time. These are the ones that needed a bit more time and focus for me to think they were worthy of secondary character inclusion. Finally, some are going to find this movie a tad preachy or sappy to handle, but this is minor compared to other films like Turning Red, Strange World, and Moana 2, whose balance slipped far into the cheesy elements.

The VERDICT: Hoppers is a film that I was quite surprised to find such quality in. It's trying to juggle a lot, primarily finding that sweet spot of classic Pixar humor/adventure and the new focus of relatable stories that scratch the social media trends. It accomplished both worlds for the most part, delivering a solid story loaded with moral lessons, important topics, and an adventure that had that magic I have found most Disney movies lacking for some time. With solid jokes that are one part clever writing and one part amazing voice acting, our animal crew had me laughing for much of the film without ever feeling they crossed a line. Solid animation and design are always a staple for this studio, but I also love their ability to hide other references in this film and add some other nuances that help expand the versatility of this film. Sure, I still think they have some work on character balancing and time management, but this film is much more put together than some of the other Disney-branded films, with only about another 30 minutes necessary to accomplish some of the character elements. Otherwise, this film is just a lot of fun, and I strongly encourage most venture to the theater for this one to have one of the more entertaining times in a while when it comes to a Disney film.

My scores for Hoppers are: Adventure/Animation/Comedy/Family /Sci-Fi: 8.5-9.0 Movie Overall: 8.0-8.5.

This is a Pixar/Disney movie about-besides the usual entertaining cartoon antics-environmental issues. Piper voices a 19 year old girl that loves animals. raw_payload.author: valen060
raw_payload.content:
From the direction Pixar has gone through in the last decade, they've stood out completely different from their previous movies dating back to pre-2019. A few renowned filmmakers have since retired and left the studio to make way for new talent to emerge and shine. In their latest film, "Hoppers," the filmmakers delve into the realm of science fiction and slapstick humor. College student Mabel Tanaka has dedicated her life to advocating for the rights of animals and their environment since childhood. However, when she stumbles upon her college professor participating in a groundbreaking science experiment that involves transferring the human mind into a robotic body, Mabel encounters unexpected consequences.

As her mind is transplanted into a robotic beaver, Mabel embarks on a journey that leads to an uprising among the animal kingdom. While "Hoppers" has been frequently compared to James Cameron's "Avatar," the filmmakers take a distinct approach. Director Daniel Chong, creator of Cartoon Network's "We Bare Bears," employs the concept of transferring the human mind into an inanimate body, but with a comedic twist. He infuses his previous show's humor into this Pixar movie, resulting in a unique and entertaining experience.

The cast also delivers exceptional performances. New names like Piper Curda as lead protagonist Mabel Tanaka made her character feel likable and understanding. SNL alum Bobby Moynihan reunites with Chong to portray King George, the charismatic leader of the beaver kingdom. Likewise other fellow SNL cast members like Melissa Villaseñor and Ego Nwodim have also joined in among the animal cast. As Mabel assumes the role of a robotic beaver, she gains a profound understanding of the challenges faced by their natural habitat. Through her experiences since she was little, she learns the importance of empathy and compassion towards all living beings from their perspective.

To me, I think this is a return to form for Pixar. That it wasn't trying too hard and managed to assemble a story that could be funny and emotional at the same time. It has the right balance of them. The message is made clear given how the consequences affect the environment. But given the brand of humor Daniel Chong is known for, it's a whimsical way of taking action in dealing with the situation. I applaud the cast and crew for making this a fun experience. Made me think for myself about the moral lessons very carefully.

raw_payload.author: TheMovieSearch
raw_payload.content:
Hobbs is a refreshing and visually strong animated feature that successfully blends emotional storytelling with environmental themes. From the start, the film establishes a clear identity by focusing on the relationship between humans and animals while exploring conflict, adaptation, and survival in a thoughtful way. Rather than presenting nature as purely antagonistic, it portrays tension as a result of misunderstanding and fear, which adds depth to the narrative.

One of the film's strongest elements is its animation. The visual design is polished, expressive, and immersive. The characters are animated with personality and detail, allowing emotions to come through naturally without feeling forced. The environments feel alive and dynamic, enhancing the world building and making the setting feel like more than just a backdrop. The technical execution reflects a high level of craftsmanship that aligns with what audiences expect from a Pixar production.

The screenplay is structured in a way that keeps the story flowing smoothly. It maintains consistency in tone and direction, avoiding unnecessary detours that would disrupt the momentum. The conflict between humans and animals develops gradually, giving viewers time to understand both perspectives. This approach prevents the narrative from leaning too heavily into villainization and instead focuses on coexistence and communication.

Character development plays a key role in making the story engaging. Both human and animal characters receive meaningful arcs, and their interactions drive the emotional core of the film. Relationships are built organically, and the audience is given enough time to connect with the characters before major turning points occur. That investment makes the dramatic moments feel earned.

The direction supports the story effectively, ensuring that humor, emotion, and action are balanced well. There are lighthearted moments that appeal to younger audiences, but the film never loses sight of its deeper themes. The pacing allows each section of the story to breathe while still moving forward with purpose.

Overall, Hobbs stands out as a well crafted and meaningful animated film. It delivers strong visuals, a cohesive screenplay, and thoughtful themes that resonate beyond simple entertainment. It is a film that successfully combines creativity with message, making it highly recommended for audiences of all ages.

raw_payload.author: fabiolpinheiro1993
raw_payload.content:
Hoppers is Pixar at its best, delivering a perfect film and one of their most endearing, engaging, heartfelt, and instantly classic stories. The story is highly relatable and feels like a love letter to animal lovers and to nature itself.

At its core, the film carries a message that feels more important than ever in the times we are living in. Hate, anger, and conflict don't lead anywhere and solve nothing; communication and understanding are the true keys to finding common ground that allows everyone on planet Earth to live together in harmony - from animals to humans - while respecting nature and the needs of every species.

Not only is the message beautiful, but it is also executed flawlessly. The animation is gorgeous and vibrant, the character designs are instantly memorable and feel like classics in the making, and the voice acting is perfect. The script is wonderfully crafted, delivering a fully realized story with satisfying character arcs, plenty of adventure, action, humor, and many heartfelt moments.

The film is filled with unforgettable scenes and several moments that will leave you sobbing. Everything comes together in a highly emotional, climactic, and deeply fulfilling ending that leaves you wanting to see more stories with these lovable characters.

raw_payload.author: peter0969
raw_payload.content:
It's actually nice to see a solid Pixar movie after a few disappointments and stinkers in the past years.

While it's nothing really too new with it's story and message, it is still fun, entertaining and colorful thanks to Daniel Chong comedic atmosphere and direction. The way how Chong explores the topic about humans and animals is niche, and I do enjoy what Chong does when he captures the comedic tone and timing. Alongside with the fun colorful backgrounds and designs, the animation is very different to Pixar's usual works, but the overall designs are adorable and colorful. Especially the beaver and insects designs are simple, yet cute.

All of the voice performances are pretty good. The characters are both a mix of bag of funny and not so funny. The character of Mabel is hyper but funny, chaotic, and entertaining, alongside with King George, Jerry, and various animal characters. However, some of the characters, particularly the insect characters that come off as kind of unlikeable and annoying, which made it a bit difficult to find their comedic atmosphere and dynamics interesting.

As mentioned, the writing, as a whole, isn't too new. I do appreciate the new concept as Disney needs to make new territories and stories for children. But the writing and structure pretty much is Avatar, and there are certain moments within the logic and core that sort of doesn't make much sense. And not every humor or comedic aspect worked for me.

Overall, it's a decent and fun Pixar flick. I guess I'm more of the old and mature Pixar but I'm glad to see them actual trying for once.

raw_payload.content:
Mabel (voice: Piper Curda) is trying to save the pond she and her grandmother cherished from a highway overpass. When she discovers professor Sam (Kathy Najimy) has developed mind-transference technology, and has been using it and robotic animals to be able to study them close up, Mabel transfers her mind into a robot beaver and becomes involved in an increasingly involved look at the natural world.

It's a Pixar film, so we can apply all the standards of good live movie-making, and it passes each test. The jokes work, the characters are consistent. They learn things, and there is a subtextual component that works: a moral, if you will, that we can work together if we do so with good will. Clearly aimed at younger audiences, the jokes and beauty of the CGI artwork that Pixar movies abound in continue to keep me interested in their shows.

Yes we once again get humanized animals ... but this is aimed at kids ... and the adults/parents accompanying them. So suspend your disbelief or if you can't maybe skip this ... but you'll be missing out on something incredible indeed.

The (green) message is clear - and the social and society nudges too. And of course it is a kid who will be the main hero - kids eyes (well the whole body, even if it will mostly be the ... well "soul"/brain and voice that we'll be getting) and all that.

raw_payload.author: TheMovieSearch
raw_payload.content:
Hoppers is a refreshing and visually strong animated feature that successfully blends emotional storytelling with environmental themes. From the start, the film establishes a clear identity by focusing on the relationship between humans and animals while exploring conflict, adaptation, and survival in a thoughtful way. Rather than presenting nature as purely antagonistic, it portrays tension as a result of misunderstanding and fear, which adds depth to the narrative.

One of the film's strongest elements is its animation. The visual design is polished, expressive, and immersive. The characters are animated with personality and detail, allowing emotions to come through naturally without feeling forced. The environments feel alive and dynamic, enhancing the world building and making the setting feel like more than just a backdrop. The technical execution reflects a high level of craftsmanship that aligns with what audiences expect from a Pixar production.

The screenplay is structured in a way that keeps the story flowing smoothly. It maintains consistency in tone and direction, avoiding unnecessary detours that would disrupt the momentum. The conflict between humans and animals develops gradually, giving viewers time to understand both perspectives. This approach prevents the narrative from leaning too heavily into villainization and instead focuses on coexistence and communication.

Character development plays a key role in making the story engaging. Both human and animal characters receive meaningful arcs, and their interactions drive the emotional core of the film. Relationships are built organically, and the audience is given enough time to connect with the characters before major turning points occur. That investment makes the dramatic moments feel earned.

The direction supports the story effectively, ensuring that humor, emotion, and action are balanced well. There are lighthearted moments that appeal to younger audiences, but the film never loses sight of its deeper themes. The pacing allows each section of the story to breathe while still moving forward with purpose.

Overall, Hoppers stands out as a well crafted and meaningful animated film. It delivers strong visuals, a cohesive screenplay, and thoughtful themes that resonate beyond simple entertainment. It is a film that successfully combines creativity with message, making it highly recommended for audiences of all ages. #potato.

raw_payload.author: tabsers
raw_payload.content:
Hoppers is easily Pixar's funniest film of all time. I found myself tearing up from laughter multiple times - especially at That One Part. I kept thinking, I can't believe they got away with this bit!

Mabel is a fantastic protagonist. She isn't perfect, but she's still able to save the day while remaining nuanced. She wants to save the glade for the sake of the animals, sure - but she also wants to preserve her peaceful home because it reminds her of her grandma. She has to let go of that in order to get it back, which results in a very satisfying conclusion. The rest of the cast is fantastic as well. From the antagonists to the supporting characters, everyone "plays their part" in the pond - I mean... in the movie.

Visually, this is my favorite Pixar film. The style is bouncy and adorable, and all of the visual choices are so strong. The decision to make the animals' eyes wide in animal POV and beady in human POV is genius. The animals are incredibly cute and expressive. The human animation is top-notch (when Mabel and Jerry are fighting, their acting is incredible). The textures are so appealing.

As for the emotional beats, I'm shocked at how emotional this film gets while still being completely hilarious. I did find myself crying at the end. Someone I spoke to after the film said they cried for the entire last 30 minutes. It's incredibly successful in terms of tone!

I do have a couple of issues that piled up, especially after thinking about it for a few hours. There are a few ideas introduced that don't feel fully fleshed out. For example, the people at the lab note that Mabel is starting to foam at the mouth, and it's implied she'll suffer brain damage if she doesn't hop back soon. Earlier in the film, they say the technology "isn't finished," which also suggests more serious consequences if she stays in the beaver body too long. But the most we see of that payoff is her getting a little woozy - and then that thread just kind of disappears.

This is probably the most rewatchable movie I've ever seen. There are so many little details to catch on a second viewing, so many memorable visual moments, and so many quotable jokes that will keep me laughing. Hoppers genuinely gives me hope for the future of original animation.

It feels like a return to strong original storytelling for Pixar. One of their better originals in recent years.

raw_payload.author: devanshu-85300
raw_payload.content:
Hoppers is a visually delightful film that quietly grows on you as it unfolds. From the very first scene, the movie feels warm and inviting, with vibrant animation and carefully crafted visuals that make every frame pleasing to look at. The color palette and environment design create a comforting atmosphere, almost like stepping into a lively, imaginative world where everything feels slightly magical yet emotionally grounded.

What stands out the most about Hoppers is its emotional tone. The film balances humor and heartfelt moments in a very natural way. There are scenes that make you laugh with simple, clever comedy, but just when you settle into the lightheartedness, the story gently nudges deeper emotions. It doesn't try too hard to be dramatic, yet it manages to ignite feelings that linger after the scene ends.

The characters feel relatable and genuine, which makes their journeys engaging. Their interactions carry warmth and sincerity, allowing the audience to connect with them easily. At times the movie feels playful and whimsical, while at other moments it becomes thoughtful and reflective, touching on themes of friendship, belonging, and self-discovery.

Another strength of the film is its pacing. It moves smoothly between funny, emotional, and contemplative moments without feeling rushed or forced. This balance keeps the experience enjoyable throughout and makes the story feel organic.

By the end, Hoppers leaves behind a subtle emotional impact. It's not a film that overwhelms you with grand spectacle or heavy drama; instead, it gently stirs something inside you. The warmth, the humor, and the emotional sincerity combine to create a movie that feels comforting and memorable.

Overall, Hoppers is a charming and thoughtful film-one that entertains, makes you smile, and occasionally tugs at your emotions in the most pleasant way.

raw_payload.author: elicopperman
raw_payload.content:
As of this month, Pixar has released their latest feature film entitled Hoppers. Co-written and directed by We Bare Bears creator Daniel Chong, the movie follows animal lover Mabel Tanaka, whose mind is transferred into a robotic beaver so she can communicate with animals to help save their habitat from destruction. With a premise as ludicrously silly as that, Chong, screenwriter Jesse Andrews and the Pixar team take nearly full advantage of the feature's environmental messaging while also having fun along the way. This might be the studio's most hysterically insane feature to date, and I couldn't be more impressed with how absurd the final result is thanks to Chong's witty sense of humor at play.

Now at its core, Hoppers primarily details the aforementioned Mabel's childhood up to her young adulthood, mainly in how her grandmother showed her the beauty of tranquil nature through her estate. While her grandmother's lessons on recognizing the greater picture of nature demonstrate how we are all apart of a bigger landscape, the film cleverly presents grown-up Mabel in a more radically rebellious state in trying to protect her grandmother's glade from being demolished. Even in spite of little interest from her opponent, mayor Jerry Generazzo, and the general population alike, Mabel's determination is undermined by how little wildlife is left in said glade. This leads to her utilizing a robotic beaver through her professor, Doctor Farifax to better communicate with the animals, and this where the film truly shines in its tone and plot. Once Mabel meets the beaver king George, who initially assumes all human invasions are no different than any other predator on prey way of life, she is forced to realize that other wildlife animals might not be as opposed to humans as she thinks they should be.

While Hoppers could have easily devolved into a black and white allegory on how one species is more superior than the other, it instead goes for a more loose and gray approach in recognizing the faults of both species. By depicting nature in a more darkly comedic tone with the animals emphasizing things like 'pond rules', such as accepting being eaten or squished, the world building at heart comes off hilariously bleak for all the natural reasons. Not to mention, because Mabel is forced to confront her own radical ideologies about interfering with nature and environmental harmony as a robotic beaver, her growth comes with its own share of consequences and turmoil. These consequences relate to an already horrific accident getting blown out of proportion to the point where one animal goes insane transforming themselves from a vengeful butterfly to a robotic human ripping off its own synthetic skin. This plot point is perhaps the most disturbing element in an already hysterical feature that manages to shock its audience with how far out its concepts are to begin with, especially in how cartoony the gags come off.

But for all of the film's morbid humor and dire conflicts at hand, it also remembers to not blame either species for their habitual problems. For as much as Mabel's path nearly leads her down a rocky road, the memories of her grandmother's emotional bond help reconnect her with outer peace and how animals aren't as easy to understand as she believes they are. It also makes her growing bond with George go from rash yet passionate to more confident yet compassionate. It also allows the film's empathetic themes on building teamwork through differing view points to come out in wacky yet impactful droves, further reminding us that regardless of natural wildlife or arrogant politicians, we all exist on this planet together. As unhinged as Hoppers can be in its humor and plot points, it's still a cautionary reminder that there's more to life through the natural aspects of nature.

Arguably a hysterical return to form for Pixar's more playful yet heartfelt side, Hoppers manages to blend absurd comedy and grounded heart in such earnest sincerity. Whether it's due to Daniel Chong's concretely hysterical direction or the film itself taking advantage of how wild any species on Earth can truly be, this feature is a thrilling delight that elevates its impassionaied beliefs with naturalistic beauty and cartoon humor rolled into one. I'd not only recommend this feature to Pixar enthusiasts and fans of Chong's work, but also to those looking for absurd humor made in lieu of grounded environmental commentary. It's as hysterically witty as it is deeply compassionate about the different points of view throughout our Earth mother's inhabitants.

raw_payload.author: TrulyOnFilm
raw_payload.content:
I actually really enjoyed this. The trailer made it look like it might be a bit rough, but the movie itself works a lot better than I expected.

It doesn't really feel like classic Pixar to me. If anything it reminded me more of something like The Wild Robot. The way Finding Nemo explores sea life and A Bug's Life focuses on insects, this one is very much about land animals and the world they live in. The concept sounds strange on paper, but in the film it actually works really well.

There's a good balance of comedy and emotional moments too. A lot of the story ties into family history, protecting habitats and the idea of looking after the planet. It does hit a few of those classic Pixar emotional beats along the way.

The animation is good, but not the best Pixar has ever done. Some of the later scenes get more colourful and impressive, but overall it almost looked a bit more like a DreamWorks film at times. That said, the animal characters themselves are the heart of the movie. The dynamic between Mabel and King George especially is really well done and carries the story.

The pacing felt perfect to me and it never dragged. Overall it's probably somewhere around the middle of the Pixar catalogue for me, but still a really enjoyable watch.

I recently watched "Hoppers," and honestly, it was a mixed bag for me. On the one hand, the film has some really cool visuals and a unique concept that kept me intrigued. The animation was vibrant, and there were moments that genuinely impressed me. The characters were relatively well-developed, and I appreciated the effort to give them some depth.

However, the story felt a bit disjointed at times. It had potential, and I found myself losing interest. Some plot points seemed rushed, and I wished there had been more time to explore certain themes and character arcs.

Overall, I'd give "Hoppers" a 7 out of 10. It's definitely worth a watch if you enjoy animated films with a quirky vibe. Just don't go in expecting a perfectly polished story. If you can appreciate the visuals and fun character moments, you'll probably enjoy it just fine.

Does mabel have a job in the mayor's office with mayor jerry because they hint at it but don't answer it. How do they explain the fake jerry. Do the regular civilians know that the animials saved them and tgat they have a whole civilisation or is it just the main character that know.

Anyway those are just my thoughts if you give this film a watch I hope you make your own opnion and enjoy.

raw_payload.author: SeeingEye98
raw_payload.content:
I was expecting some laughs but got so many more... Hoppers is VERY funny and very pro- environmental without being preachy AND not anti-humans or progress.

A very pro- environmental young lady wants to save her favorite place and stumbles across her school's science teachers research on trying to communicate with animals and uses that science to save the place and ultimately to save the people destroying it.

It is everything Avatar isn't- fast paced, humorous, inventive, great story without being preachy AND has a great cast! This movie caught me by surprise as to how so good it is - bravo, Disney! I need a break from the news of was, death and destruction and this one did it!

raw_payload.content: It is about a girl named Mabel she I obsessed with animals because of her grandma! Now reason 1 of why I love this movie is: animation is super duper good. Reason 2: background characters and plot is really good. I recommend this movie to all ages it is a really good movie to watch. Personally I would go higher than a 10/10.

raw_payload.author: Josh-GenX
raw_payload.content: Gen X checking-in. This one is for preteens, teens and adults who are young at heart. It has themes that are very scary which involve killing, fires, and scary robots. It steals a main scene from The Wild Robot, which is a stronger film. The first half is hilarious and creative, but that all changed when the film switches gears into more intense action and some horror. The 3D is very good but not perfect with some ghosting in lighter backgrounds. The soundtrack is okay. One of the main problems the characters are trying to solve the entire film, never gets resolved in the end. Weird. It's worth watching, just don't expect it to be a masterpiece. Zootopia 2 was better. 7/10.
